title: Emergency Guide: Sudden Hind Leg Paralysis in Cats (Saddle Thrombus)
meta_desc: Sudden back leg paralysis in cats is a life-threatening emergency often caused by a blood clot. Act fast and contact an emergency vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind leg paralysis in cats is a life-threatening medical emergency often caused by a blood clot, requiring immediate veterinary intervention to manage extreme pain and restore circulation.

What causes sudden hind leg paralysis in my cat?

Sudden loss of mobility in a cat's hind legs is most commonly caused by Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), also known as a 'Saddle Thrombus.' This occurs when a blood clot, often originating from an underlying heart condition, travels through the aorta and lodges where the artery branches to the back legs, instantly cutting off blood supply and oxygen to the limbs.

Is sudden loss of mobility in a cat’s back legs a medical emergency?

  • Seek emergency veterinary care immediately, as this is a high-priority, life-threatening medical emergency.
  • Observe if your cat is experiencing extreme, excruciating pain or vocalizing loudly.
  • Act quickly because tissue is being deprived of blood; every minute counts to manage pain and attempt to restore circulation.

How can a photo of my cat's paws help with veterinary triage?

  • Provide a clear photo of your cat’s back paws to help triage professionals instantly recognize a Saddle Thrombus event.
  • Check if the paw pads have turned a pale, white, or bluish-purple color due to the lack of blood flow.
  • Share these photos so the veterinary team can assess the severity of the circulation issue and prepare for your arrival before you even walk through the door.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Arterial thromboembolism, most commonly resulting in saddle emboli at the aortic trifurcation, is a frequent cause of sudden hind limb paralysis in cats. Underlying conditions that result in left atrial enlargement, such as cardiomyopathies, hyperthyroidism, or congenital heart disease, predispose cats to this condition due to intracavitary thrombi formation in the left atrium. Clinical signs include pain, paresis or paralysis of the hind limbs, reduced or absent arterial pulses (femoral or pedal), cool extremities, and firm, swollen gastrocnemius muscles. Doppler ultrasonography helps evaluate blood flow in the distal aorta and femoral arteries. Management consists of analgesics, nursing care, and treatment of any underlying disease. Anticoagulants, such as unfractionated heparin or low-molecular-weight heparin, are used to reduce continued thrombus formation. The long-term prognosis is guarded.

Chapter: Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1245)

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms of a Saddle Thrombus?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include sudden inability to use the back legs, paws that feel cold to the touch, pale or blue-tinted paw pads, and intense vocalization due to pain.

faqs.question: Can my cat recover from hind leg paralysis?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends on the severity of the clot and the underlying heart disease. While some cats can regain mobility with intensive care, the prognosis is often guarded.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat as calm as possible in a secure carrier. Do not attempt to massage the legs, as this can be extremely painful and may dislodge the clot further.
